title: Error Handling Practice
instructions: Complete functions that handle errors gracefully using try-except blocks.
hints: Use try-except to catch specific exception types
Multiple exception types can be caught in one except clause using tuple
Return the default value in the except block

testCases.description: safe_divide should return correct result for valid division
safe_divide should return None when dividing by zero
testCases.validation: assert safe_divide(10, 0) is None, 'Should return None for division by zero'

testCases.description: safe_int_convert should convert valid string to int
testCases.validation: assert safe_int_convert('42') == 42, 'Should convert string to int'

testCases.description: safe_int_convert should return 0 for non-numeric strings
testCases.validation: assert safe_int_convert('hello') == 0, 'Should return 0 for invalid conversion'

testCases.description: safe_list_access should return None for out-of-range index
testCases.validation: assert safe_list_access([1, 2, 3], 10) is None, 'Should return None for out-of-range index'

testCases.description: safe_dict_get should return default when key is missing
testCases.validation: assert safe_dict_get({'a': 1}, 'z', 'default') == 'default', 'Should return default for missing key'

description: Build robust Python functions by implementing error handling with try-except blocks. Practice catching ZeroDivisionError, ValueError, TypeError, IndexError, and KeyError to create functions that fail gracefully and return safe default values instead of crashing.
